fields.description:
## Why

Epic 716f has drained the module-size allowlist to zero — every over-cap module is split and no `src/rebar/**/*.py` exceeds 800 LOC. Now retire the grandfathering ESCAPE HATCH so the 800 cap becomes absolute, and LOCK the limit so it cannot be changed through the normal contribution process (only an admin force-submitting past the gate can land a limit change). Per the operator decision, this is a CI-gate-in-code mechanism (no Gerrit ACL).

This is Phase 2a (the enforcement SWAP). It cannot also delete the allowlist/ceilings files, because the CI Module-size gate runs from MAIN's workflow against each change's tree — main's CURRENT (allowlist-based) gate would break reading the deleted `ceilings` file on this change's own CI. So 2a rewrites the gate + tests and ADDS the limit single-source, but KEEPS the now-empty allowlist/ceilings files; a follow-up (Phase 2b) deletes them once the new gate is live on main.

## What

1. Add `.github/module-size-limit.txt` containing just `800` — the SINGLE SOURCE of the limit, read by both the CI gate and the contract test.
2. Rewrite the `Module-size gate` step in BOTH `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` and `.github/workflows/test.yml` (keep the step NAME 'Module-size gate') to: (a) read `LIMIT` from the limit file (fail if missing or not a positive int); (b) ABSOLUTE cap — fail if any `src/rebar/**/*.py` exceeds $LIMIT (no allowlist, no exemptions); (c) LIMIT LOCK — compare against origin/main's limit and fail with an 'must override' ::error:: if it differs (bootstrap: origin/main has no limit file yet, so it does not fire on THIS change); (d) MISSING-FILE GUARD — fail if the limit file is absent. The step reads NEITHER the allowlist NOR the ceilings file in EITHER workflow.
3. Replace `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py`: drop the allowlist/ceilings sync assertions; read the cap from the limit file; assert no `src/rebar` file exceeds it and the file parses to a positive int.
4. Update `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` to a signature the NEW gate contains in both workflows (`.github/module-size-limit.txt`).
5. KEEP the (now-empty) allowlist + ceilings files in this change (Phase 2b deletes them), so main's current gate stays green on THIS change's CI.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `.github/module-size-limit.txt` exists and contains `800` (verify: `test "$(cat .github/module-size-limit.txt)" = 800`)
- [ ] In BOTH workflow files the gate reads the limit file and reads NEITHER the allowlist NOR the ceilings file (verify, checks EACH file: `for f in .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml .github/workflows/test.yml; do grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' "$f" || exit 1; ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' "$f" || exit 1; ! grep -q 'module-size-ceilings' "$f" || exit 1; done && echo OK` prints OK, exit 0)
- [ ] The limit-lock override message is present in BOTH workflows (verify: `for f in .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml .github/workflows/test.yml; do grep -q 'must override' "$f" || exit 1; done && echo OK` prints OK, exit 0)
- [ ] The gate FAILS when the limit file is MISSING (verify: run the gate step against a tree with no `.github/module-size-limit.txt` → exit 1 with a 'must not be removed' message)
- [ ] A simulated limit change fails the lock; unchanged passes (verify: the lock snippet with BASE=800 LIMIT=900 → exit 1 with the override message; BASE=800 LIMIT=800 → exit 0)
- [ ] `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py` asserts no src file over the file-sourced cap and passes (verify: `pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py -q`)
- [ ] `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` passes with the updated signature (verify: `pytest tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py -q`)

## Notes
Phase 2b (follow-up) deletes the orphaned allowlist/ceilings files, prunes the docs/architecture.md offenders table, and updates AGENTS.md — kept separate because the gate must stop reading those files (this change) BEFORE they can be deleted. E5: the gate is shell (oracle = running it against crafted trees, per the ACs); the contract + parity tests are the Python oracle.
